name: Chicha
cultural_notes: Traditional corn beer of the Andes, dating back to the Inca Empire. Chicha de jora is the most common variety. Often homemade and central to festivals.
manufacturing_notes: Made by fermenting corn (jora), sometimes with saliva for enzymes in traditional methods
safety_notes: Low ABV but hygiene standards vary significantly in rural settings
Unfiltered nature can be heavy on the digestive system
risk_factors: Potential for active fermentation causing bloating
recommendations: Look for established community Chicherias (indicated by a red flag/flower on a pole)
Avoid if you have a very sensitive stomach for unfiltered ferments

name: Makgeolli
cultural_notes: Korea's oldest alcoholic beverage, a milky rice wine. Traditionally enjoyed by farmers. Experiencing revival among young people. Often served in a bowl.
manufacturing_notes: Unfiltered, so it's cloudy and continues fermenting
safety_notes: Low ABV makes it very manageable
Contains lactobacillus and fiber, often considered a 'healthy' alcohol
risk_factors: High carbonation can cause rapid fullness
recommendations: Gently shake or swirl before pouring to mix the sediment

name: Sake / Nihonshu
cultural_notes: Japanese rice wine (actually a brewing process more like beer). Central to Shinto rituals and celebrations. Quality ranges from table sake to premium junmai daiginjo.
manufacturing_notes: Polished rice fermented with koji mold
safety_notes: ABV is much higher than beer (similar to wine)
Very smooth flavor can lead to drinking faster than intended
risk_factors: Rapid intoxication if treated like beer
recommendations: Sip from small cups (ochoko) to regulate pace
Match the temperature (hot/cold) to the season for the best experience

name: Palm Wine (Tuak, Toddy, Matango, Emma, Nsafufuo)
origin: West Africa, Southeast Asia, South Asia
cultural_notes: Sap tapped from palm trees that ferments naturally within hours. Central to social life in palm-growing regions.
manufacturing_notes: Tapped from palm trees, ferments rapidly
safety_notes: Potency increases throughout the day as it ferments
Risk of adulteration in commercial street markets
risk_factors: Unpredictable alcohol levels in late-day batches
recommendations: Consume as early in the day as possible for the freshest, lowest-ABV experience

name: Marula Wine
cultural_notes: Traditional wine made from marula fruit. Elephants famously love the fermented fruit. Harvest season brings communities together.
manufacturing_notes: Fruit ferments naturally, often in containers
safety_notes: Usually high in Vitamin C from the fruit
risk_factors: Wild yeast can cause minor digestive upset in visitors
recommendations: Best enjoyed during the peak harvest season (Jan-March)

name: Sato
cultural_notes: Thai rice wine, similar to Lao-Lao but fermented rather than distilled. Popular in Isan region.
manufacturing_notes: Fermented glutinous rice with starter culture
safety_notes: High sugar content can lead to a quick buzz and potential headache
ABV is higher than most beers
recommendations: Drink with spicy Isan food to balance the sweetness
Hydrate well due to the sugar

name: Toddy (Pakistan/Sindh)
cultural_notes: Traditional palm wine of Sindh, despite restrictions. Tapped from date palms.
safety_notes: Primary risk is legal and social due to local laws
risk_factors: Potential for poor sanitation in hidden production
recommendations: Only consume if offered in a trusted private residence
Be extremely discreet and aware of local laws and customs
